title: Municipal food-waste collection versus home composting
subtitle: How council in-vessel processing and anaerobic digestion compare to a home heap for nutrient recovery and practical use
excerpt: UK councils collected 5.5 million tonnes of food waste in 2022, processed mostly by anaerobic digestion or in-vessel composting. The nutrients and carbon in that food waste leave the household permanently. A home compost heap returns roughly 40 percent of the organic matter from raw vegetable waste back to your soil. The two systems serve different material types: understanding which to use for what reduces both collection lorry trips and home-heap contamination.
sourceNotes: WRAP UK food waste collection statistics 2022. Defra separate food waste collection consultation 2023. Renewable Energy Association anaerobic digestion fact sheets. WRAP home composting guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A process in which micro-organisms break down organic material in the absence of oxygen, producing biogas (used for energy) and digestate (used as a soil amendment).
A commercial composting process carried out in enclosed containers or tunnels at controlled temperature, allowing food waste including cooked food and meat to be composted quickly and at scale.

content.text: When cooked meat, fish, dairy, and cooked food go into the council food-waste caddy, they are sent either to

. Both process materials that a home heap should not handle. Cooked food, meat, and dairy attract rats and pests in a standard garden heap; without sufficient heat, their decomposition also generates odour and pathogen risk. The council system handles these categories safely at scale.
What each system does with the materials

In anaerobic digestion, food waste is converted to biogas (typically 55 to 65 percent methane) and digestate. The biogas is burned to generate electricity or upgraded to biomethane and injected into the gas grid. The digestate is applied to agricultural land. In in-vessel composting, food waste is shredded, mixed with structural material (wood chip), and processed at 55 to 65 degrees C for 14 to 28 days to kill pathogens. The resulting compost is used in horticulture or land restoration, not returned to the householder. Both processes capture the carbon and nutrients in a usable form, but neither returns material to the householder's garden.
What the home heap does better

A home compost heap processes raw fruit and vegetable waste, egg shells, tea bags, coffee grounds, garden clippings, and paper and card, and returns the finished compost directly to the soil that grew the plants. The nutrient loop is closed at household scale. WRAP estimates that a home composter with a 340-litre bin and consistent use diverts 150 to 200 kg of organic waste from the residual bin per year and produces 30 to 60 kg of compost for the garden. The embodied transport cost is zero. The limitation is material acceptance: raw vegetable matter only, no cooked food, no meat, no dairy in a standard heap.
Home heap: raw vegetable and fruit peelings, plastic-free tea bags, coffee grounds, egg shells, raw garden waste (not woody), paper and card (torn small), grass clippings in layers.
Council caddy: cooked food, meat, fish, bones, dairy, bread, pasta, rice. Cooked starchy foods are fine for AD and in-vessel composting but problematic in a home heap.
Neither system reliably handles: heavily waxed card, compostable plastic packaging. Most UK councils do not accept compostable plastics; check your council's guidance before adding to the caddy.

attrs.symptom: Council food-waste caddy smells very strongly within two days

content.text: Line the caddy with newspaper or a certified compostable liner. Add a small amount of coffee grounds or dry shredded paper on top of cooked food before closing the lid: these absorb moisture and slow fermentation. Empty the caddy at least twice a week in summer. If the collection is weekly and the smell is unmanageable between collections, refrigerate or freeze cooked food waste until collection day.

attrs.symptom: Home heap is not heating up despite adding green material

content.text: Home heaps do not need to heat up to produce useful compost: cold composting works, it just takes 6 to 12 months rather than 6 to 8 weeks. If the goal is hot composting (which kills weed seeds and pathogens), the heap needs to be at least 1 cubic metre, reach a carbon to nitrogen ratio of 25:1 to 30:1, and be turned every 3 to 5 days. Most garden heaps are too small for effective hot composting: accept cold-process timescales or scale up to a large open heap.
